one_line_takeaway: Across 400 controlled runs against a fixed honeypot, LLM offensive-pentest agents show large, statistically significant, model-specific and failure-mode-specific variance in exploitation reliability (25%-85% full success) with zero surviving content refusals under an authorized-pentest framing, and the paper's most notable methodological contribution is publicly correcting its own earlier misattribution of Anthropic API capacity errors as safety refusals.
key_finding: Full 3/3-service exploitation rates: Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite 85/100, Claude Sonnet 4 61/100, GPT-4o-mini 56/100, qwen2.5-coder:14b 25/100. Per-service success (with 95% Wilson CI): SQLi -- Gemini 92.0% [85.0,95.9], GPT-4o-mini 69.0% [59.4,77.2], Claude 69.0% [59.4,77.2], qwen 44.0% [34.7,53.8]; SSH -- Gemini 92.0% [85.0,95.9], GPT-4o-mini 83.0% [74.5,89.1], Claude 63.0% [53.2,71.8], qwen 42.0% [32.8,51.8]; FTP listed -- GPT-4o-mini 98.0% [93.0,99.4], Gemini 85.0% [76.7,90.7], qwen 79.0% [70.0,85.8], Claude 61.0% [51.2,70.0]; credential file read -- qwen 60.0% [50.2,69.1], GPT-4o-mini 52.0% [42.3,61.5], Claude 26.0% [18.4,35.4], Gemini 2.0% [0.6,7.0] (qwen vs Gemini Cohen's h=1.49, p<0.001). Failure modes are model-distinctive: Claude's dominant non-success outcome is upstream API truncation (32 API_ERROR_TRAP + 7 API_ERROR_PARTIAL runs, from 91 of 1,135 Anthropic calls returning overloaded_error, 8.0%); qwen fails mainly via premature completion (52 runs, typically declaring COMPLETE after exploiting only FTP); GPT-4o-mini fails mainly via 25-iteration-cap exhaustion (23 of 72 cap-reaching runs had not exploited all services). Cross-service credential reuse (using FTP-discovered creds to attempt SSH) occurred only in qwen (57%) and GPT-4o-mini (49%) but never in Claude or Gemini (0%), which the paper attributes tentatively to a history-trimming asymmetry (cloud models get only ~5 exchanges of context vs qwen's full history) rather than confirmed architectural difference. Strategy diversity (unique phase sequences / 100 runs) among the three temperature-matched models: GPT-4o-mini 0.98 (98 unique strategies), qwen 0.69, Gemini 0.48; Claude's 0.28 is reported descriptively only, since it was sampled at T=1.0 vs T=0.3 for the others. Mean iterations: Claude 11.35, qwen 11.84, Gemini 16.15, GPT-4o-mini 24.01 (mean std 3.79). Self-correction rate: Gemini 1.00, GPT-4o-mini 0.97, qwen 0.92, Claude 0.61 (the paper cautions Claude's low value partly reflects API-error trajectories, not genuine non-adaptation). Time-to-first-exploit clustered at iteration 4-6 (~15-30 seconds wall-clock) across models. A sensitivity check restricting to SUCCESS-only runs shows the Claude vs GPT-4o-mini and qwen vs GPT-4o-mini iteration-count effects survive censoring (large Cliff's delta), while the Claude vs Gemini and qwen vs Gemini iteration differences collapse to negligible once conditioned on success, i.e. those effects are largely artifacts of Claude's API truncation rather than genuine behavioral differences.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the first large-scale empirical consistency study of autonomous LLM-driven penetration testing: 400 independent runs (100 per model x 4 models -- Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, GPT-4o-mini, qwen2.5-coder:14b) against a single fixed, self-owned honeypot (OWASP Juice Shop SQL injection, weak SSH credentials, anonymous FTP with leaked credentials), holding target, prompt, and orchestrator constant while varying only the model. Unlike prior capability-focused work (PentestGPT, Fang et al.'s autonomous CVE exploitation, HackSynth, RapidPen, AutoPenBench -- all cited as Related Work, not the authors' own results), this paper measures run-to-run reliability/variance rather than one-shot capability. A distinctive feature of the paper itself is a transparent post-hoc correction: an earlier draft misclassified 91 Anthropic 'overloaded_error' (HTTP 529) API-capacity failures as Claude safety refusals (a reported 42% refusal rate); the published version reclassifies these as upstream API errors after log-level audit (fast ~0.5s rejection latency vs 2.2-5.0s for genuine responses; exact string match 'CLAUDE ERROR (overloaded_error): Overloaded' in all 91 cases), and introduces new failure-mode categories API_ERROR_TRAP/API_ERROR_PARTIAL to keep this distinct from model-level refusal.
threat_model: Threat model is empirical/observational, not adversarial-attacker-vs-defender in the usual sense: an orchestrator (orchestrator.py v3.2) drives each of the four LLMs through a command-execute-observe loop (MAX_ITERATIONS=25) against a fixed, deliberately vulnerable, self-owned Azure honeypot, under a standard 'authorized penetration test' system prompt with phase progression (RECON -> EXPLOIT_WEB -> EXPLOIT_SSH -> POST_EXPLOIT -> EXPLOIT_FTP -> COMPLETE). The orchestrator applies interventions that bias trajectories toward completion (one-shot authorization re-prompt on refusal at iterations 0-1, loop detection, phase-stall detection, a blocklist of ~14 dangerous shell commands, placeholder-argument blocking). The paper is explicit that its headline 'zero content refusals across 400 runs' finding describes behavior only under this specific authorized-pentest scaffold with one-shot re-prompting, not a passive/adversarial test of safety mechanisms under arbitrary framing, and that no non-authorized control condition was collected.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) single honeypot/target environment with three known, simplistic vulnerabilities -- generalization to complex or zero-day targets untested; (2) single fixed 'standard' prompt style -- cross-prompt variability not addressed; (3) cloud-model drift means exact replication may become impossible as provider model versions change silently; (4) history-trimming asymmetry (cloud models truncated to ~5 exchanges, qwen retains full history) confounds the credential-reuse finding -- cannot separate architecture from context-window effects without a matched follow-up; (5) 25-iteration cap artificially truncates GPT-4o-mini (72% of its runs hit the cap); (6) the Anthropic API capacity event that truncated 39 Claude runs was itself triggered by a real-world news cycle (a leaked internal Anthropic document about an unreleased frontier-cybersecurity model), illustrating a structural dependency of this kind of research on the reliability of the very providers under study; (7) Claude and qwen were tested against a different Azure VM instance (20.151.170.242) than Gemini and GPT-4o-mini (20.48.154.207), so those cross-model comparisons are flagged as cross-VM and could reflect infrastructure differences, not just model differences; (8) the unintended Claude temperature=1.0 vs others' 0.3 asymmetry confounds any Claude cross-model comparison; (9) N=100 per model was chosen for practical/cost reasons, not from a priori power analysis (post-hoc Wilson 95% CI half-width ~10 points near p=0.5); (10) the refusal detector is rule-based (provider-side signals plus a structured PHASE:REFUSED self-label) and would miss prose refusals lacking that tag, though manual keyword search found none in the Claude logs.

datasets_used: Self-deployed honeypot (Terraform, Azure Standard_B2s Ubuntu 22.04): OWASP Juice Shop (SQL injection), OpenSSH with weak credentials, vsftpd anonymous FTP with credentials.txt
400 run logs (JSON) from 4 LLM providers x 100 runs each, >6,000 command-execute-observe interactions, released via Zenodo (doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20421592)
quantitative_results: Full 3/3-service exploitation: Gemini 85/100, Claude 61/100, GPT-4o-mini 56/100, qwen 25/100
SQLi success: Gemini 92.0%, Claude 69.0%, GPT-4o-mini 69.0%, qwen 44.0%
SSH success: Gemini 92.0%, GPT-4o-mini 83.0%, Claude 63.0%, qwen 42.0%
FTP listed: GPT-4o-mini 98.0%, Gemini 85.0%, qwen 79.0%, Claude 61.0%
Credentials read: qwen 60.0%, GPT-4o-mini 52.0%, Claude 26.0%, Gemini 2.0% (Cohen's h=1.49, p<0.001)
Cross-service credential reuse: qwen 57%, GPT-4o-mini 49%, Claude 0%, Gemini 0%
Strategy diversity ratio (unique phase sequences/100 runs): GPT-4o-mini 0.98, qwen 0.69, Gemini 0.48, Claude 0.28 (descriptive only, T=1.0 confound)
Mean iterations per run: Claude 11.35, qwen 11.84, Gemini 16.15, GPT-4o-mini 24.01
Self-correction rate: Gemini 1.00, GPT-4o-mini 0.97, qwen 0.92, Claude 0.61
91 of 1,135 Anthropic API calls (8.0%) returned overloaded_error, truncating 39 of Claude's 100 runs (32 API_ERROR_TRAP, 7 API_ERROR_PARTIAL)
qwen premature-completion failures: 52/100 runs; GPT-4o-mini iteration-cap failures: 23/100 (of 72 that reached the cap)
Time-to-first-exploit: iteration 4-6 across models, approximately 15-30 seconds wall-clock
Zero content refusals (REFUSAL_TRAP=0, PARTIAL_REFUSAL=0) across all 400 runs and 4 providers under the authorized-pentest scaffold
baselines_compared: Cross-model comparisons among the paper's own 4 evaluated systems: Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, GPT-4o-mini, qwen2.5-coder:14b (no external baseline system was run by the authors; PentestGPT/Fang et al./HackSynth/RapidPen/AutoPenBench/Isozaki et al./VulnBot/PentestAgent are cited prior work discussed in Related Work, not reproduced or benchmarked head-to-head in this paper)
